question: Reinforcement learning (RL) is learning to act in an MDP whose dynamics are not given in advance. (a) Compared with solving an MDP (L13/L14), what is unknown in RL, and what does the agent get to observe instead? (b) Distinguish PASSIVE from ACTIVE learning. (c) Describe how passive Adaptive Dynamic Programming (ADP) turns experience into a value function.
key_concepts: In RL the transition model P(s' | s, a) and the reward R(s) are UNKNOWN. The agent does not get the MDP up front; it only observes experiences -- tuples <s, a, s', r>: it is in state s, takes action a, lands in s', and receives reward r. It must learn to act well while (or from) interacting
Passive learning: the policy pi is FIXED and the goal is just to evaluate it -- learn V^pi(s) for every state from the experiences generated by following pi
Active learning: the agent also CHOOSES its actions, so it must both learn good value estimates AND decide what to do (this forces the exploration-vs-exploitation problem)
Passive ADP is MODEL-BASED: from experiences it estimates the model -- R(s) <- observed reward at s; counts N(s,a) and N(s,a,s'); estimated transition P_hat(s' | s, a) = N(s,a,s') / N(s,a)

It then plugs the estimated R_hat and P_hat into policy evaluation (the Bellman equations for a fixed policy) and solves for V^pi -- exactly or by a few iterative sweeps -- re-solving as the estimates improve with more data

question: An active RL agent faces the exploration-vs-exploitation dilemma. (a) State the dilemma in one sentence and explain why a purely greedy (always-exploit) agent can get stuck. (b) Describe THREE exploration strategies, giving the rule or formula for each. (c) For epsilon-greedy and for softmax, say what happens at the two extremes of their parameter.
key_concepts: Dilemma: EXPLOIT = take the action that looks best under the current estimates (reap known reward) vs EXPLORE = try other actions to improve the estimates. A purely greedy agent can lock onto a suboptimal action because it never gathers the data that would reveal a better one -- its estimates for untried actions stay wrong
epsilon-greedy: with probability epsilon take a uniformly random action (explore), otherwise take the greedy action argmax_a Q(s,a) (exploit)
Softmax / Boltzmann: pick actions in proportion to their value through a temperature T: P(a) = exp(Q(s,a)/T) / sum_{a'} exp(Q(s,a')/T) -- better actions are more likely, but all get some probability

Optimistic initialization / exploration function: assume the best about rarely-tried actions. Replace a value estimate u by f(u, n) = R+ (an optimistic bonus) if the state-action has been visited fewer than N_e times, else u -- this drives the agent to try under-explored actions first

Extremes: epsilon-greedy with epsilon -> 0 is pure exploitation (greedy), epsilon -> 1 is pure random exploration. Softmax with T -> 0 becomes greedy (all mass on the best action), T -> infinity becomes uniform random. Common practice: DECAY epsilon or T over time so the agent explores early and exploits later

question: Q-learning and SARSA are model-free temporal-difference (TD) control methods. (a) After one transition <s, a, r, s'>, write the TD error and the general update Q(s,a) <- Q(s,a) + alpha * delta, and say what alpha does. (b) Give the Q-learning and SARSA targets and explain how they differ. (c) Define on-policy vs off-policy, classify each method, and say which is safer for high-stakes online learning and why.
key_concepts: TD idea: after a transition, pretend it was certain and nudge Q(s,a) toward a one-step 'target'. Update: Q(s,a) <- Q(s,a) + alpha * delta, where delta (the TD error) = target - Q(s,a). The learning rate alpha in (0,1] sets how far we move toward the target each step (small alpha = slow, stable; often decayed as a state-action is visited more)
Q-learning target: r + gamma * max_{a'} Q(s', a') -- uses the value of the GREEDY next action. So delta = r + gamma max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)

SARSA target: r + gamma * Q(s', a'), where a' is the action ACTUALLY taken next in s' by the current (e.g. epsilon-greedy) policy. So it uses the move it will really make, not the greedy one -- hence the name SARSA (s, a, r, s', a')

On-policy = learns the value of the SAME policy it uses to act (behavior policy = target policy); off-policy = learns about a DIFFERENT target policy than the one it behaves with. SARSA is ON-policy (its target uses the action it will take); Q-learning is OFF-policy (its target uses the greedy action regardless of what it does)

SARSA is safer for high-stakes online learning: because its target reflects the exploratory actions it will actually take, it learns to avoid catastrophic outcomes along the exploration path (e.g. it stays away from a cliff). Q-learning learns the optimal greedy policy but its target ignores the cost of exploratory mistakes, so it can behave riskily while still exploring

question: A decision tree is a supervised classifier that is 'nothing more than a nested if-then-else' learned from data. (a) Describe the four structural parts of a decision tree (root, internal node, edge, leaf) and how you CLASSIFY a new example. (b) The recursive learner keeps splitting the data; give the THREE base cases at which it stops and what it returns in each. (c) Why is 'no examples left' handled differently from 'no features left'?
key_concepts: Structure: the ROOT and every INTERNAL NODE test one input feature; the EDGES out of a node are labeled with that feature's possible values; each LEAF holds a predicted target class. The tree predicts a single discrete target

Classify: start at the root and follow the edge matching the example's value for the tested feature; repeat at each internal node until you reach a leaf; output that leaf's class. The path taken is literally a chain of if-then-else tests
Base case 1 -- all examples at the node share the same class: return that class (a pure leaf)
Base case 2 -- no features left to test (all used up) but the examples still disagree (noisy data): return the MAJORITY class of the examples at this node

Base case 3 -- no examples reach the node (a feature value never appeared in training): return the majority class of the PARENT node. It differs from case 2 because there are no local examples to take a majority over, so we fall back on the parent's data; case 2 handles noise, case 3 handles unseen feature-value combinations

question: The open question in tree learning is WHICH feature to test at each node; ID3 answers it with information gain. (a) Give the entropy formula and its values for a pure node and a 50/50 node. (b) Define information gain IG using H_before and H_after, and state ID3's greedy rule. (c) For the tennis data (9 Yes, 5 No, H_before = 0.940 bits), IG(Outlook) = 0.247 and IG(Humidity) = 0.151 -- which becomes the root, and is ID3 guaranteed to find the best overall tree? (d) Name one way trees OVERFIT and one fix, and briefly contrast random forests with gradient boosting.
key_concepts: Entropy of a class distribution: I(P(c_1), ..., P(c_k)) = - sum_i P(c_i) log_2 P(c_i), measured in bits of uncertainty. A pure node I(1,0) = 0 (no uncertainty); a balanced node I(0.5, 0.5) = 1 (maximum uncertainty). (Gini impurity G = 1 - sum_i P(c_i)^2 is a cheaper, log-free alternative with the same spirit.)

Information gain: H_before = entropy of the node's labels; H_after = the WEIGHTED average entropy of the children after splitting on a feature, H_after = sum_i ((p_i + n_i)/(p + n)) * I(child_i); IG = H_before - H_after (expected reduction in uncertainty). ID3's greedy rule: at each node choose the feature with the LARGEST information gain, then recurse

For the tennis data, Outlook wins the root because IG(Outlook) = 0.247 > IG(Humidity) = 0.151 (a bigger drop in entropy). ID3 is GREEDY -- locally best feature at every node -- so it is fast and usually good but NOT guaranteed to find the globally smallest / best tree

Overfitting: grown to purity a tree can memorize the training set (in the worst case one leaf per noisy example) and generalize poorly. Fixes: PRE-pruning (cap max depth, require a minimum number of examples per node, or a minimum gain to split) or POST-pruning (grow the full tree, then collapse branches that don't improve held-out accuracy)

Ensembles combine many trees. RANDOM FORESTS (bagging): train many trees in parallel, each on a bootstrap sample (n rows drawn with replacement) and a random subset of features, then majority-vote / average -- this reduces VARIANCE. GRADIENT BOOSTING: add shallow trees in SEQUENCE, each correcting the residual errors of the ones so far -- this reduces BIAS and (via XGBoost / LightGBM) is a top performer on tabular data
